original_abstract:
The three-dimensional architecture of carbohelicenes has long captivated molecular chemists, leading to their widespread use in diverse scientific fields. In recent years, their intense circularly polarized luminescence has made them prime candidates for developing advanced optical materials. While researchers have successfully developed enantioselective syntheses for various fused carbo- and heterohelicenes, a direct catalytic method for creating lower, non-fused carbo[n]helicenes (n = 4–6) has remained elusive.

We describe an efficient, palladium-catalyzed enantioselective C–H arylation that grants simple and general access to these lower carbo[n]helicenes by employing a unique bifunctional phosphine-carboxylate ligand. Computational mechanistic studies reveal a complex stereoselectivity model where both the C–H activation and the reductive elimination steps influence the final enantiomeric ratio. Our analysis suggests that the observed enantio-induction is driven by a synergistic combination of non-covalent interactions and specific steric repulsions between the substrate and the ligand during the two decisive reductive elimination pathways. Finally, we provide a systematic evaluation of the photophysical and chiroptical properties of the resulting enantioenriched [n]helicenes, establishing a clearer link between their helical structure and optical performance.
humanized_abstract:
The three-dimensional architecture of carbohelicenes has fascinated molecular chemists for many years, and these molecules have been widely used in many areas of science. Lately, their high circularly polarized luminescence (CPL) has also made them candidates for design of new optical materials. While enantioselective syntheses have been developed for several classes of fused carbo- and heterohelicenes, there is no direct catalytic method known for preparing the lower non-fused carbo[n]helicenes (n = 4–6).

We developed an efficient palladium-catalyzed enantioselective C–H arylation method for preparing lower carbo[n]helicenes using a bifunctional phosphine-carboxylate ligand. Mechanistic simulations suggested a complex model influencing the enantio-outcome of the reactions at the C–H activation and reductive elimination steps. In the latter stages, the enantio-induction is the result of a delicate balance between non-covalent (NC) interactions and the steric repulsions between the substrate and the ligand along the transition states of the two main reductive elimination pathways. Finally, we examined the photophysical and chiroptical properties of enantioenriched [n]helicenes, to gain some insight into the structural and optical relationships that exist among these molecules.

We developed an efficient palladium-catalyzed enantioselective C–H arylation method for preparing lower carbo[n]helicenes using a bifunctional phosphine-carboxylate ligand. Mechanistic simulations suggested a complex model influencing the enantio-outcome of the reactions at the C–H activation and reductive elimination steps. In the latter stages, the enantio-induction is the result of a delicate balance between non-covalent (NC) interactions and the steric repulsions between the substrate and the ligand along the transition states of the two main reductive elimination pathways. Finally, we examined the photophysical and chiroptical properties of enantioenriched [n]helicenes, to gain some insight into the structural and optical relationships that exist among these molecules.

In recent years, their intense circularly polarized luminescence has made them prime candidates for developing advanced optical materials.
